Quarterly portfolio recap

In this environment, the fund performed in line with the Russell 1000 Growth index's 1.12% gain. Coming off a quarter where the market was increasingly concentrated and focused on the key AI beneficiaries, the fourth quarter did allow other areas of the market to participate and was generally broader. During the quarter, the fund fared well on a relative basis in the health care and industrials sectors. These positives were offset by lesser results in the information technology and consumer discretionary sectors.

After lagging earlier in the year, health care stocks began to bounce back to close out 2025 on a solid rally. Our holdings in biotechnology firms Argenx and Insmed (INSM) and pharma leader Eli Lilly all delivered strong returns and were relative outperformers. Argenx is a global biotechnology company focused on the development of innovative antibody-based therapies for the treatment of severe auto immune diseases and cancer. The company continued to execute well, led by strong sales of the firm's Vyvgart therapy for treatment of generalized myasthenia gravis (gMG) and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP).

Insmed shares continued to advance, as the firm's launch of its therapy Brinsupri, which is the first approved treatment for non-cysticfibrosis bronchiectasis, a rare lung condition, got off to a strong start. Brinsupri was approved by the FDA during the previous quarter.

Eli Lilly continued to take market share in the diabetes and obesity markets, led by the company's Mounjaro and Zepbound franchises. Investors also grew increasingly optimistic that the firm's oral obesity pill, which has the ability to fuel additional growth, could be approved in early 2026.

Additionally, our holding in health care equipment provider Intuitive Surgical delivered a strong return and was an outperformer.

Our holding in liquid cooling technology leader Vertiv (VRT) was a relative outperformer in the industrials sector. Vertiv announced another impressive quarter, beating virtually every metric, and revised its guidance higher, pointing to significant organic growth in 2026. The firm's technology continues to be in high demand for next-generation AI data centers.

In terms of detractors, the portfolio lost ground in the information technology sector, as many software names continued to be under pressure as investors weighed whether they would be AI beneficiaries or see their business models disrupted. Our holdings in ServiceNow and cybersecurity leaders Palo Alto Networks and CrowdStrike (CRWD) all pulled back and detracted from relative results.

Shares of ServiceNow sold off, despite the company announcing a strong quarter in which it exceeded expectations on its federal government business unit; the government shutdown was an overhang to sentiment and continued adoption of ServiceNow's AI-powered Now Assist platform. Even with these positives, sentiment remained challenged around software leaders, as the market continued to rotate away to more value-oriented names. Additionally, ServiceNow announced an all-cash acquisition of cyber security firm Armis — ServiceNow's largest deal ever — which also weighed on the name late in the year.

Similar to ServiceNow, CrowdStrike pulled back despite a generally solid quarter, in which the firm beat sales estimates, executed strongly across its business and raised guidance.

Additionally, not owning Apple (AAPL) was a headwind this quarter, as its shares rebounded after selling off earlier in the year on tariff concerns and delays to AI enhancements across its product lines.

In the consumer discretionary sector, our holdings in South American ecommerce leader MercadoLibre and auto parts retailer O'Reilly Automotive both declined and weighed on relative returns. MercadoLibre delivered a mixed quarter, as the firm continued to execute its growth strategy with strong sales, especially in Brazil and Mexico. Despite continued growth, sentiment stalled on the name as higher costs weighed on margins.

Outlook

With the broader market near all-time highs, the volatility that was experienced throughout 2025 may persist into the new year. But as we have said in the past, many times investors discount how resilient the U.S. economy is, and thinking long term, we continue to believe that will be the case. We will monitor developments closely, including employment levels, inflation and potential fiscal and monetary responses and their impact on the market and portfolio.

When thinking about our current portfolio, we continue to be invested in very durable secular growth names versus generally more cyclical and commoditized names. We continue to have meaningful exposure to the transformational theme of AI, which we believe is still in the early innings and will play out over years and not just the next few months. In consumer stocks, which may bear the brunt of higher prices and inflation from tariffs, we have specifically looked to names that provide the most "bang for the buck" and can even benefit from trade-down effects from higher-priced good or delayed purchases.

Lastly, one of the biggest areas of differentiation of our portfolio versus the Russell 1000 Growth Index remains our overweight to the health care sector, where we continue to find differentiated names that benefit from the long-tail demographic trends of an ageing population and may provide more defensive characteristics than other parts of the market.

In the end, we will remain patient and look for opportunities, as we continue to believe that these select high-growth names look very compelling for long-term investors.

data.title: Lindsay Corporation: It's Too Soon For An Upgrade
data.description:
Over the last several months now, Lindsay Corporation (LNN) has had a bit of a rough time. Even though the company ended 2025 with higher revenue, profits, and cash flows than it had in 2024, a big decline in backlog, combined with concerns about the economy more broadly, proved problematic. The picture was made worse when, for the first quarter of 2026, management announced a decline in all of these metrics year over year. As a result of this, the stock has actually fallen 12.5% since I last reaffirmed it as a Hold candidate back in September of last year. For context, the S&P 500 (SP500) is down just 1.4%.

Even though I fully expect market conditions to worsen before they get better, I do think that the company is getting closer to being attractive enough to justify a Buy rating. But I'm not ready to make that jump just yet. Based on how the stock is valued, combined with expectations of further weakening moving forward, remaining cautious is the right choice.

Of course, my assessment of the matter could change as new data comes in. But right now, expectations point toward continued weakness. You see, before the market opens on April 2nd, the management team at the company is expected to announce financial results for the second quarter of the firm's 2026 fiscal year. As we approach that date, the thought is that revenue and profitability will decline. So unless we see something big and unexpected occur that happens to be a positive for the company, I believe that maintaining it as a Hold candidate is the most appropriate course of action.

Checking in on Lindsay Corporation

Perhaps the best place to start when it comes to analyzing Lindsay Corporation right now would be to talk about exactly what analysts expect from the upcoming earnings release. As I mentioned already, before the market opens on April 2nd, the firm is going to be announcing financial performance for the second quarter of its 2026 fiscal year. The thought right now from analysts is that revenue will come in at $171.4 million. Although that's a nice chunk of change, it would actually represent a decline of 8.4% compared to the $187.1 million that the company reported a year earlier.

This drop in revenue is expected to be accompanied by weakening profits and cash flows also. Take earnings per share as an example. Analysts currently believe that it will generate $1.69 per share in profits. That is a big drop from the $2.44 reported at the same time last year, and it would mean a drop in net income from $26.6 million to $18.1 million. Analysts have not provided estimates when it comes to cash flows. But in the table above, you can see what cash flows were for the second quarter of 2025. In all likelihood, declining revenue and profits will translate to weaker cash flows as well.

At first glance, this might seem peculiar. After all, as the chart above illustrates, revenue actually jumped from 2024 to 2025. As it expanded, profitability followed suit. Net income grew from $66.3 million to $74.1 million. Even more impressive was operating cash flow, which shot up from $95.8 million to $132.9 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we do get a smaller but still impressive increase from $90.1 million to $99.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $97.4 million to $108.6 million.

The weakness for the company really began to show up in revenue and profitability in the first quarter of 2026. The chart above shows a decline in every metric that I looked at for that time. Given the nature of the business, it's not unexpected to see some volatility. I could point to multiple examples here. But perhaps the most interesting one involves its Road Zipper technology. For the uninitiated, this is a barrier system that management developed whereby T-shaped concrete and steel barriers are connected in order to form a continuous wall. The firm also has a barrier transfer machine as part of this system that can move the barrier laterally across the pavement.

The goal here is to be able to quickly and easily erect and move barriers that are necessary for things like road work. But by management's own admission, revenue associated with this part of the company is incredibly volatile and often hinges on a small number of sizable contracts that come and go.

I would be lying to you if I said that was the only reason why the firm has seen some volatility. To understand exactly what is going on here, it would be helpful to dig deeper. In the chart below, you can see financial performance for the last three fiscal years, as well as for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025, associated with the company’s Infrastructure segment. This is the same part of the firm that includes its Road Zipper System. But it also consists of other offerings like crash cushions, specialty barriers, road marking and safety equipment, rail products, and the like.

This is actually the best part of the company right now, given its overall financial trajectory in recent years. Even during the first quarter of 2026, revenue and profits were higher than they were a year earlier. But a lot of what this segment does relies on state and federal government funding. For instance, management pointed out the $110 billion of incremental federal funding for things like roads, bridges, and other transportation projects under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. That funding is expected to run through September of this year. Unfortunately, there have been some issues, such as inflation associated with material prices and labor costs, that have offset this. But with only 88% of the funds having been committed to States and only 58% of them having been reimbursed to the states, there is still plenty of capital to go around.

By comparison, the Irrigation equipment has become more of an issue. After seeing revenue and profits increase from 2024 to 2025, we saw a decline in both in the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time the previous year.

According to management, both North America and international markets experienced declining irrigation revenue on a year-over-year basis, with the former down 4% while the latter was down 15%. In North America, the drop was because of a reduction in unit sales volume that more than offset higher average selling prices. And when it comes to the international market, the company saw weakness because of Brazil and Western Europe, on top of lower project revenues in the MENA (Middle East and North Africa) region.

It appears as though high-interest rates and credit challenges have proven to be problematic in Brazil specifically. But I do think that here at home is more relevant. After all, North America was responsible for 55.7% of segment revenue during that time.

A big challenge for a company such as Lindsay Corporation, at least so far as it comes to the Irrigation segment, is the fact that net farm income has been in an interesting spot. According to the USDA, it did total $154.5 billion in 2024. That was up from the $127.5 billion reported a year earlier. But there are some caveats here that need to be explained.

A big reason for this surge stemmed from the fact that, year over year, federal government funding jumped $20.4 billion. Without this, net farm income would have risen a more modest 5.2% year over year. But when we start looking at a breakdown of this data, it does become disconcerting. Net farm income includes cash receipts from not only crops but also animals and products as well. Year over year, crop-related cash receipts dropped from $244.9 billion to $238.1 billion. This marked the third consecutive year of declines in this regard. And in fact, you would have to go back to 2020 to see crop cash receipts come in lower than what they were last year. There's also the expectation that we won't really see actual recovery this year, with it inching up only slightly to $240.8 billion.

According to the data provided by the USDA, on an inflation-adjusted basis, total cash receipts for the industry should fall by $24.3 billion, or 4.5%, year over year. This includes a bit of a decline in crop cash receipts and a sizable one of 7.6% when it comes to animal and animal product receipts. When it comes to specific crops, we can expect this year to see an increase of 3.4%, though most other categories will see results come in either stable or down.

Management highlighted that while prices for U.S. soybeans and corn were a bit higher than they had been in prior months, they still remained well below what they were from 2021 through about half of 2024. And in Brazil, prices are higher. But because of broader economic weakness there, we are likely to experience some pressure from that market. Management mentioned in their latest earnings call that low commodity prices caused by higher output, on top of high input costs, have proven to be a challenge for the agricultural industry.

And because of those challenges, management is focusing more this year on prices and cost management, on top of operational efficiencies, in order to compete effectively while maximizing shareholder returns. This is not to say that there won't be any opportunity for growth in some respects. Management specified, for instance, that the company landed a project valued at about $80 million in the irrigation space for the MENA region. $70 million of that should be generated this year. This should also help to boost backlog, considering that the project in question came after quarterly results became official. That meant that while backlog did fall from $168.2 million in the first quarter of 2025 to $119.2 million in the first quarter of 2026, the likely effective backlog today is considerably higher.

Keeping in mind that we really don't know what to expect for the rest of the year, I merely projected our performance based on how the first quarter looked. This would give us net profits of $71.1 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $96.4 million, and EBITDA of $102.8 million. Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment.

These aren't bad multiples, but they aren't fantastic, either. Typically, I would say that they put the company into the low end of the fair value range. And relative to other similar firms, the stock looks more or less fairly valued also.

In the chart above, you can see that while only one of the five firms that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis, two of them were cheaper when it came to the other valuation metrics. And specifically, when it came to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach, a third one was tied with it.

Takeaway

All of the data present right now suggests to me that Lindsay Corporation makes for an interesting prospect. In a more normal economic environment, I might even turn a bit bullish on it. However, because of how the stock is priced, in combination with the fact that we should expect further weakening this year, I think that rating it a ‘hold’ makes a lot more sense. But if management can come out with some really fantastic results and expectations for the fiscal year, I could upgrade it.
